title: Build Your Personal Homepage
description: Create a polished personal landing page with a hero section, skills grid, project showcase, and contact form. Learn responsive design, CSS animations, performance optimization, and deploy to multiple hosting platforms.
keyPoints: Plan your site goals, audience, content strategy, and domain name before writing code
Apply design principles like visual hierarchy, typography pairing, and color theory
Build a full-width hero section with gradient backgrounds and animated text
Create an about section with bio, skill progress bars, and experience timeline
Showcase projects with card layouts, tech stack badges, and live demo links
Implement a validated contact form with email integration and spam protection
Use mobile-first responsive design with proper breakpoints
Optimize performance with Lighthouse, WebP images, font loading, and Core Web Vitals
Deploy to Vercel, Netlify, or GitHub Pages with custom domain and SSL
Add privacy-respecting analytics with Plausible or Umami

blocks.heading: Planning Your Personal Site
blocks.body: A personal homepage is your corner of the internet. Before writing a single line of code, define what you want the site to accomplish. Are you looking for a job? Freelance clients? A place to share your thoughts? The answer shapes every design decision that follows.

cards.title: Define Your Goals
cards.body: Decide whether you want to attract employers, freelance clients, open-source collaborators, or simply have a creative outlet.

cards.title: Know Your Audience
cards.body: Technical recruiters skim for keywords. Design clients want visual proof. Open-source devs want GitHub links. Tailor accordingly.

cards.title: Content Strategy
cards.body: Plan your sections: hero, about, skills, projects, testimonials, contact. Less is more — cut anything that doesn't serve your goal.

cards.title: Domain Name
cards.body: yourname.dev or firstname-lastname.com. Keep it short, professional, and easy to spell out loud.

cards.title: Design Inspiration
cards.body: Browse Dribbble, Awwwards, and other developer portfolios. Screenshot what you like and identify patterns.

cards.title: Tech Stack Choice
cards.body: For a first site, vanilla HTML/CSS/JS is perfect. You can always migrate to React or Next.js later.

blocks.title: Content-first design
blocks.body: Write your about text, project descriptions, and tagline before touching CSS. Designing around real content prevents the common trap of building a gorgeous template that says nothing.

blocks.heading: Design Principles for Developer Sites
blocks.body: Good design is invisible — visitors should absorb your content without thinking about the layout. A few foundational principles make the difference between an amateur page and a professional one.

blocks.heading: Core Design Principles
rows: Guide the eye to the most important content first
Use size, color, and spacing to make your name and tagline the first thing seen
Use two complementary fonts — one for headings, one for body
Try Inter + serif for body, or a monospace heading with clean sans-serif body
Choose a primary accent color and build a palette from it
Use a tool like coolors.co. Stick to 2-3 colors plus neutrals
Empty space is not wasted space — it creates breathing room
Add generous padding between sections (80-120px) and around text blocks
The first viewport should instantly communicate who you are
Hero section with name, role, and one clear call-to-action
Repeating patterns create a professional feel
Use the same border radius, spacing scale, and color for similar elements

blocks.description: A typography system using CSS custom properties with a modular scale for consistent sizing.
A full color palette with light and dark mode using CSS custom properties.

blocks.heading: Project Structure
blocks.body: Organize files logically from the start. Even a simple static site benefits from separating concerns into folders.

blocks.title: Create the Project Structure
blocks.body: Start with a clean folder. Keep styles, scripts, and images in their own directories.
blocks.code:
my-homepage/
  index.html
  css/
    style.css
    typography.css
    colors.css
    animations.css
  js/
    main.js
    contact.js
  images/
    profile.webp
    projects/
      weather-app.webp
      task-manager.webp
  favicon.svg

blocks.heading: Hero Section
blocks.body: The hero is your digital handshake. It should fill the entire viewport, immediately communicate who you are, and give the visitor one clear next action. A gradient background, your name with a text animation, a profile photo, and a call-to-action button are the essential ingredients.

blocks.title: Build the HTML Shell with Semantic Elements
blocks.body: Use semantic HTML for accessibility and SEO. The page has a fixed nav, a hero, and content sections.
blocks.code:
JD
  • About
  • Skills
  • Projects
  • Contact

Hello, I'm

Jane Doe

Front-End Developer & UI Designer

I build accessible, performant websites that look great on every device. Currently focused on React, TypeScript, and design systems.

View My Work Get In Touch
↓

blocks.description: Hero section styles with a gradient background, animated text, and profile photo with a decorative ring.

blocks.heading: About Section with Bio and Experience Timeline
blocks.body: The about section humanizes you beyond a list of technologies. Include a short bio, a professional photo, and an experience timeline that shows your career progression.

blocks.description: About section with a two-column layout: bio on the left, experience timeline on the right.
blocks.code:
About Me

I am a front-end developer with 3 years of experience building web applications. I started with WordPress, moved to vanilla JavaScript, and now work primarily with React and TypeScript. I care deeply about accessibility, performance, and clean code.

When I am not coding, you will find me hiking, reading sci-fi novels, or contributing to open-source projects.

2024 — Present

Front-End Developer at TechCorp

Building React component libraries and design systems.

2022 — 2024

Junior Developer at StartupXYZ

Built customer-facing dashboards and landing pages.

2022

Bootcamp Graduate

Completed a 12-week full-stack JavaScript bootcamp.

blocks.description: Styles for the about section two-column layout and the vertical experience timeline.

blocks.heading: Skills with Progress Bars
blocks.body: A skills section lets visitors quickly assess your technical range. Progress bars provide a visual indicator of proficiency, and animating them on scroll makes the page feel polished.

blocks.description: Skills section with categorized skill bars that animate into view on scroll.
blocks.code:
Skills & Technologies

Frontend

Backend & Tools

blocks.description: Animated progress bars with labels and percentage indicators.
JavaScript to render skill bar DOM, animate fills on scroll, and handle the Intersection Observer.

blocks.heading: Projects Showcase
blocks.body: Project cards are the centerpiece of any developer homepage. Each card needs a screenshot, title, short description, the tech stack as badges, and links to both the live demo and the GitHub repository.

blocks.description: Project data and rendering with tech stack badges, image, and links to live demo and source code.
Project card styles with hover overlay revealing demo and source links.

blocks.heading: Contact Form with Validation and Email Integration
blocks.body: Your contact form is useless if it does not actually deliver messages. We will build a form with real-time validation, then connect it to an email delivery service. We cover three options: Formspree (no backend), EmailJS (client-side SDK), and Resend (API-based).

blocks.description: Contact form with accessible labels, honeypot spam protection, and real-time validation placeholders.
blocks.code:
Get In Touch

Have a project in mind or just want to say hello? I would love to hear from you.

blocks.description: Contact form validation with real-time error clearing and submission to Formspree.
Alternative email delivery using EmailJS — sends from the browser with no backend.
Server-side email delivery using the Resend API — more reliable than client-side solutions.
Contact form styles with focus states, error highlighting, and success/error message formatting.

blocks.heading: Responsive Design — Mobile-First Approach
blocks.body: Over 60% of web traffic comes from mobile devices. Building mobile-first means starting with the smallest screen and adding complexity for larger ones. This approach naturally produces cleaner CSS because you add features rather than remove them.

blocks.heading: Common Breakpoints
rows: Single column, stacked elements, hamburger menu
Full layout with sidebar or multi-column grids

blocks.description: Mobile-first responsive breakpoints for the entire page layout.
CSS-only hamburger menu using a checkbox hack for zero-JavaScript mobile navigation.
Toggle handler for the mobile hamburger menu.

blocks.heading: CSS Animations and Scroll Effects
blocks.body: Subtle animations make a page feel alive without being distracting. Use the Intersection Observer API to trigger fade-in animations as elements scroll into view, and add micro-interactions on hover for buttons and cards.

blocks.description: Scroll-triggered animation keyframes and utility classes.
Generic Intersection Observer that adds .visible to any .fade-in element when it enters the viewport.

blocks.title: Respect prefers-reduced-motion
blocks.body: Some users experience motion sickness from animations. Always wrap animation CSS in a @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) query that disables or minimizes movement. This is both an accessibility requirement and a best practice.

blocks.heading: Dark Mode Toggle
blocks.body: Dark mode is expected on developer sites. We implement it with a data attribute on the root element, CSS custom properties for all colors, and localStorage to persist the user's preference.

blocks.description: Dark mode toggle that respects system preference on first visit and persists choice to localStorage.

blocks.heading: Performance Optimization
blocks.body: A personal homepage should load in under two seconds. Google ranks fast sites higher, and visitors bounce from slow ones. Here is a systematic approach to hitting a Lighthouse score above 90 in every category.

blocks.heading: Core Web Vitals Targets
rows: Time until the biggest visible element renders
Optimize hero image, preload fonts, use CDN
Time until the page responds to first click/tap
How much the layout jumps around during load
Set explicit width/height on images, reserve font space
Responsiveness to all interactions, not just first
Break up long tasks, use requestAnimationFrame

blocks.heading: Image Optimization Checklist
items: Convert all photos to WebP format (80% smaller than JPEG with no visible quality loss)
Use AVIF for hero images if browser support is acceptable (even smaller than WebP)
Set explicit width and height attributes on every to prevent layout shift
Use loading="lazy" on all images below the fold
Use the <picture> element with srcset to serve different sizes for different screens
Keep hero images under 200 KB — compress with squoosh.app or sharp
Use SVG for icons and logos (infinitely scalable, tiny file size)
Consider using placeholder blur-up technique for large images

blocks.description: Responsive image loading with WebP/AVIF formats, srcset for multiple sizes, and lazy loading.
Preload critical fonts and use font-display: swap to prevent invisible text during load.
blocks.code:
<head>
  <!-- Preload the most critical font weight -->
  <link
    rel="preload"
    href="/fonts/inter-var-latin.woff2"
    as="font"
    type="font/woff2"
    crossorigin
  />

  <style>
    /* Self-host fonts for better performance than Google Fonts */
    @font-face {
      font-family: 'Inter';
      src: url('/fonts/inter-var-latin.woff2') format('woff2');
      font-weight: 100 900;
      font-display: swap;  /* Show fallback font immediately */
      unicode-range: U+0000-00FF; /* Latin characters only */
    }
  </style>
</head>

blocks.title: Self-hosting vs Google Fonts
blocks.body: Self-hosting fonts eliminates a DNS lookup and connection to fonts.googleapis.com. Download font files from google-webfonts-helper, put them in your /fonts folder, and reference them with @font-face.

blocks.heading: Lighthouse Performance Checklist
rows: Compress images, minify CSS/JS, defer scripts, preload fonts
Semantic HTML, alt text, focus styles, color contrast, skip link
HTTPS, no console errors, correct doctype, CSP headers
Meta description, title tag, Open Graph, structured data, mobile viewport

blocks.heading: Deployment Options
blocks.body: Static sites can be deployed for free on multiple platforms. Each has strengths depending on your workflow.

blocks.title: Deploy to GitHub Pages
blocks.body: GitHub Pages hosts static sites for free from any repository. Push your code and enable Pages in settings.
blocks.code:
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit: personal homepage"
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/my-homepage.git
git branch -M main
git push -u origin main

# Then: Settings > Pages > Source: Deploy from branch > main / root

blocks.title: Custom domain on GitHub Pages
blocks.body: Add a CNAME file with your domain name to the repo root. Update your DNS with an A record pointing to 185.199.108.153. GitHub provides free SSL for custom domains.

blocks.title: Deploy to Vercel
blocks.body: Vercel provides instant deployments with preview URLs for every push. Install the CLI or import directly from GitHub.
blocks.code:
# Option 1: CLI
npm i -g vercel
vercel

# Option 2: Import from GitHub
# Go to vercel.com > New Project > Import your repo
# Vercel auto-detects static sites and configures the build

blocks.title: Deploy to Netlify
blocks.body: Netlify offers drag-and-drop deployment or Git integration. It also supports form handling without a backend.
blocks.code:
# Option 1: CLI
npm i -g netlify-cli
netlify deploy --prod --dir=.

# Option 2: Drag and drop
# Go to app.netlify.com/drop
# Drag your project folder onto the page

# Option 3: Git integration
# Connect your GitHub repo in the Netlify dashboard

blocks.title: Netlify Forms
blocks.body: Add a netlify attribute to your form tag and Netlify automatically handles submissions with spam filtering — no backend code needed. <form name="contact" netlify>

blocks.heading: Analytics — Tracking Visitors Responsibly
blocks.body: You need to know if anyone actually visits your site, which pages they view, and where they come from. But you should respect visitor privacy. Google Analytics 4 is the default, but privacy-focused alternatives are better for personal sites.

blocks.heading: Analytics Platform Comparison
rows: Collects extensive data, requires cookie consent banner
Sites that need detailed funnels and conversion tracking
No cookies, no personal data, GDPR-compliant by default
Personal sites that want simple, clean analytics
Developers who want full control and zero cost

blocks.description: Add Plausible analytics with a single script tag — no cookie consent needed.
blocks.code:
<!-- Add before </head> -->
<script defer data-domain="janedoe.dev" src="https://plausible.io/js/script.js"></script>

blocks.description: Self-hosted Umami analytics — deploy on Railway or Vercel for free.
blocks.code:
<!-- Point to your self-hosted Umami instance -->
<script defer src="https://analytics.janedoe.dev/script.js" data-website-id="YOUR_WEBSITE_ID"></script>

blocks.description: Deploy Umami to Railway with one command for free, privacy-respecting analytics.
blocks.code:
# Clone and deploy Umami to Railway
git clone https://github.com/umami-software/umami.git
cd umami

# Or deploy directly from the Umami dashboard:
# Go to cloud.umami.is/signup for a free hosted version

blocks.heading: SEO Essentials
blocks.body: Search engine optimization ensures that when someone Googles your name, your personal site appears first. For a personal homepage, the fundamentals matter more than advanced techniques.

blocks.heading: SEO Checklist for Personal Sites
items: Write a unique <title> tag under 60 characters: 'Jane Doe — Front-End Developer'
Add a <meta name="description"> under 160 characters summarizing who you are
Include Open Graph meta tags (og:title, og:description, og:image, og:url) for social sharing
Add Twitter Card meta tags for rich previews on Twitter/X
Use semantic HTML elements (header, nav, main, section, article, footer)
Add structured data (JSON-LD) for Person schema so Google shows a knowledge panel
Create a favicon.svg with your initials — it shows in browser tabs and bookmarks
Submit your sitemap to Google Search Console
Ensure all images have descriptive alt text
Use descriptive anchor text for links instead of 'click here'

blocks.description: JSON-LD structured data for Person schema — helps search engines understand who you are.
blocks.code:
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "Person",
  "name": "Jane Doe",
  "url": "https://janedoe.dev",
  "image": "https://janedoe.dev/images/profile.webp",
  "jobTitle": "Front-End Developer",
  "worksFor": {
    "@type": "Organization",
    "name": "TechCorp"
  },
  "sameAs": [
    "https://github.com/janedoe",
    "https://linkedin.com/in/janedoe",
    "https://twitter.com/janedoe"
  ],
  "knowsAbout": ["React", "TypeScript", "CSS", "Web Accessibility", "Design Systems"]
}
</script>

blocks.body: Open Graph images appear when your site is shared on Twitter, LinkedIn, Slack, and other platforms. Instead of a static screenshot, you can generate a dynamic image using @vercel/og or a simple HTML-to-image approach.

blocks.description: A simple OG image template you can screenshot at 1200x630px using a browser or automation tool.
blocks.code:
Jane Doe
Front-End Developer
janedoe.dev

blocks.heading: Footer and Final Touches
blocks.body: The footer wraps up your page. Keep it simple: copyright, a few social links, and optionally a link to the site source code to show transparency.

blocks.description: Minimal footer with copyright, social links, and a link to the source code.
blocks.code:
© 2025 Jane Doe. Built with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

GitHub LinkedIn Source

blocks.description: Footer styles with flexbox layout.

cards.title: Accessibility Audit
cards.body: Run Lighthouse and axe DevTools. Fix color contrast issues, add skip-to-content link, ensure all interactive elements are keyboard-accessible.

cards.title: Print Stylesheet
cards.body: Add @media print styles to hide the nav, show URLs next to links, and format for paper. Recruiters sometimes print portfolios.

cards.title: 404 Page
cards.body: Create a custom 404.html with navigation back to the homepage. GitHub Pages and Netlify both support this automatically.

cards.title: Progressive Web App
cards.body: Add a manifest.json and service worker to make your site installable and available offline. Use Workbox for cache strategies.

blocks.title: Your personal homepage is ready
blocks.body: You now have a fully responsive, animated, accessible personal homepage with a hero section, about page with timeline, animated skill bars, project showcase with hover overlays, a validated contact form with email integration, dark mode, SEO, performance optimization, and multiple deployment options. Update the data with your real content, push to your preferred host, and share it.

blocks.heading: Summary
blocks.body: Building a personal homepage is the best way to learn foundational web development. You practiced semantic HTML, responsive CSS with custom properties, JavaScript DOM manipulation, form validation, scroll animations with Intersection Observer, dark mode toggling, image optimization, and deployment. These skills transfer directly to every web project you will build in the future.

references.title: Don't Make Me Think, Revisited (3rd Edition)
